title: Nanocomposite fire retardants ? a review
abstract:
*Correspondence to: D. Porter, School of Chemical and Life Sciences, University of Greenwich, Wodwich, London SE18 6PF, UK.
FIRE AND MATERIALS
Fire Mater. 24, 45}52 (2000)
Nanocomposite Fire Retardants = A Review
D. Porter*, E. Metcalfe, and M. J. K. Thomas
School of Chemical and Life Sciences, University of Greenwich, Woolwich, London SE18 6PF, UK
Most 5re retardant nanocomposites are made from layered silicates and organic polymers, a variety of methods are
used in their synthesis.
The mechanism for the 5re retardancy of these composites is generally considered to be due to the structure of the
char formed during combustion, which enables the char to thermally insulate the polymer and inhibit the formation
and escape of volatiles during combustion.
Fire retardant nanocomposites require relatively low concentrations of silicates for activity, resulting in low
additional costs and weight. Improvements in the bulk physical properties of the polymer can be additional advantages
over traditional 5re retardants. Copyright ( 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
INTRODUCTION
The ever increasing use of #ammable materials in equip-
ment and furnishings places emphasis on research into
improving their "re retardant properties. Nanocom-
posites are showing great promise as e!ective "re retar-
dants for some types of materials, as a result interest in
this area of research has recently increased.
Many di!erent types of nanocomposite materials are
now available, but they all share two de"ning features:
they are made by combining two or more materials, and
these materials intermingle on a nanometer scale. This
combination may give nanocomposite materials physical
properties that are superior to the original materials used
in their construction. Nanocomposites can be made from
a wide variety of starting materials, including gases,
metals and minerals, giving a range of enhanced proper-
ties including electrical conductivity1 and superparamag-
netism2 as well as "re retardancy.
This article focuses on silicate-polymer nanocom-
posites. It explains how they are made and the likely
mechanisms of action as "re retardants. As this is a rap-
idly developing area this review only includes research
published before 1999.
The starting materials used in producing "re retardant
nanocomposites are layered silicates and polymers, pro-
ducing compounds where, for example, clay is bonded to
polystyrene.3 Silicates have been successfully used in the
past as "ller type "re retardants, sodium metasilicate
pentahydrate has been found to delay ignition and re-
duce weight loss on ignited foams4 and aluminosilicates
have been used to reinforce carbon "bre composites5
creating non-combustible materials. Silicates have also
been used as starting materials for many types of "re
retardants additives. QD polyorganosiloxanes produced
from vermiculite have "re retarding properties.6 While
some polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane macromers
undergo structural rearrangements when heated that are
accompanied by changes in char porosity and density7
a!ecting
combustion.
Polyhedral
oligomeric
silses-
quioxane macromers can be derived from silicates.8
Many groups have been involved in research into "re
retardant nanocomposites. At the forefront of this re-
search has been The Department of Materials Science
and Engineering at Cornell University and The National
Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) in Mary-
land. These institutions have made extensive contribu-
tions to the work in this area.
SILICATES USED IN FIRE RETARDANT
NANOCOMPOSITES
The silicates used in the production of the nanocom-
posite are layered, with cations loosely bonded between
sheets of oxygen and silicon. The clay montmorillonite
has been found to be the most suitable,9 but other clays
as well as natural and manmade micas are also used.
These alternatives have included lithium #uorohec-
torite,10 synthetic #uorinated mica11 and other layered
silicates.12
Clays are hydrous aluminium silicates with a layered
structure. Montmorillonite is a smectite clay, these are
clays with weak bonding between the cations, water
molecules and silicate layers.
The composition of montmorillonite varies greatly;
with aluminium replacing di!ering amounts of silicon
and divalent cations. This is compensated for by the
presence of additional amounts of hydroxyl ions and
variations in the type of cations present. As a result
montmorillonite has a high cation exchange capacity.
The clay can swell and shrink substantially depending on
the amount of water incorporated and the particular
composition of the clay, as shown in Table 1. Naturally
occurring montmorillonite consists of a random mixture
of unit cells, and the spacing between layers can vary
from 1.0 nm to 2.1 nm.
Moreover the swelling behaviour of montmorillonite
by organic molecules has been con"rmed many groups.15
It is for these reasons that montmorillonite is suitable as
a starting material for the synthesis of silicate-polymer

Figure 2. The structures of polymer-silicate nanocomposites.
nanocomposites. The nanometer-scale layers and gaps
needed to facilitate the formation of the composite by
intercalation are already present, as shown schematically
in Fig. 1.13 The mineral has a high cation exchange
capacity, allowing organophilic cations to be easily ac-
commodated and therefore the intercalation of organic
molecules is possible.
THE NANOCOMPOSITE STRUCTURES
Fire retardant nanocomposites take the form of or-
ganic}inorganic hybrids. The properties of the resulting
materials are strongly related to the structures of these
nanocomposites.
Figure 2 shows the possible structures of silicate-poly-
mer
nanocomposites.
These
can
be
described
as
intercalated, delaminated and end tethered, with the in-
tercalated and delaminated structures being the most
commonly studied. However, at the present time there is
no agreement on the preferred structure, although some
research indicates that intercalated hybrids may be more
e!ective "re retardants.10
Intercalated hybrids consist of either a single monomer
or extended polymers sandwiched between the host sili-
cate layers. This results in a well ordered multi-layered
structure comprising alternating silicate and polymer
layers.
In delaminated hybrids the silicate layers are exfoliated
and dispersed in a polymer matrix, rather like a sea of
polymer, with rafts of silicate layers contained within it.
This is a much less ordered system than the intercalated
structure.
Two types of end-tethered hybrids16 have been pro-
duced, one where the end of the polymer is attached to
the outside of the silicate sheet and the other where the
end of the polymer is attached to an exfoliated layer of
the silicate. The "rst type has not yet been reported as
having "re retardant properties. The second type is sim-
ilar to a delaminated structure with polymer surrounding
exfoliated layers of silicate. It is important to bear in
mind that more than one type of hybrid may be found in
a system, although usually only the most prevalent struc-
ture is mentioned.
SYNTHESIS
For a nanocomposite to form the silicate must be able to
accommodate the polymer. In most cases this means
replacing cations within the layers of the silicate with
organic species, turning the hydrophilic mineral into
a more organophilic host. These organically modi"ed
layered silicates are commonly made by cation-exchange
reactions between the silicate cations and an alkylam-
monium salt. The positive head of the alkylammonium
cation is attracted by the negatively charged silicate
layer,
leading
to
a
preferred
orientation
of
the
ammonium
molecule,
where
the
hydrocarbon
tail
is directed away from the silicate layer, as shown in
Fig. 3.
The methods for making intercalated or delaminated
nanocomposites vary from simple to very complex. Two
of these methods are shown in Fig. 4.
The direct polymer melt intercalation method is the
simplest and relies on the silicate being su$ciently or-
ganophilic to accept the polymer without prior modi"ca-
tion. This constraint obviously limits the range of poly-
mer-silicate systems for which this method works.17
A polyethylene oxide-silicate hybrid17 intercalate can
be made using the direct melt method. The polyethylene
oxide and the layered silicate are heated together at 803C
for 6 h. Both x-ray di!raction and di!erential scanning
calorimetry indicate that the resulting product is poly-
ethylene oxide intercalated into the silicate.
A particularly elegant synthesis is the ring opening
method.18 This can only be used for the production of
certain types of hybrid, e.g. nylon 6-clay hybrids, but has
the bene"t of allowing the structure of the nanocom-
posite to be controlled by varying the concentration of

The methods for making intercalated or delaminated
nanocomposites vary from simple to very complex. Two
of these methods are shown in Fig. 4.
The direct polymer melt intercalation method is the
simplest and relies on the silicate being su$ciently or-
ganophilic to accept the polymer without prior modi"ca-
tion. This constraint obviously limits the range of poly-
mer-silicate systems for which this method works.17
A polyethylene oxide-silicate hybrid17 intercalate can
be made using the direct melt method. The polyethylene
oxide and the layered silicate are heated together at 803C
for 6 h. Both x-ray di!raction and di!erential scanning
calorimetry indicate that the resulting product is poly-
ethylene oxide intercalated into the silicate.
A particularly elegant synthesis is the ring opening
method.18 This can only be used for the production of
certain types of hybrid, e.g. nylon 6-clay hybrids, but has
the bene"t of allowing the structure of the nanocom-
posite to be controlled by varying the concentration of
D. PORTER, E. METCALFE AND M. J. K. THOMAS
Copyright ( 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Figure 3. A preferred orientation of the ammonium ion.
Figure 4. Two methods of synthesis.17,18
montmorillonite. In this method the montmorillonite is
treated with an amino acid then dispersed in water,
e-caprolactam (the cyclic lactam used in nylon 6 produc-
tion) and concentrated hydrochloric acid are added. The
mixture is stirred for 5 min, producing an intercalated
e-caprolactam-clay hybrid, by means of a cation ex-
change reaction. In the second stage of the process, e-
caprolactam, 6-aminolauric acid and the e-caprolactam-
clay hybrid are heated together at 2603C for 6 h under
a nitrogen atmosphere at normal pressure. The rings of
the e-caprolactam are cleaved to form reactive mono-
mers, which polymerize and then react with the interca-
lated hybrid. This product is cooled and then milled,
giving pellets of nylon-6-clay. When the mass fraction of
montmorillonite used is less than 20% a delaminated
hybrid is produced, but intercalated hybrids form when
a greater percent of clay is used.18
Other synthesis methods are abundant. Melt intercala-
tion can be successful when used with many types of
polymer,19 including rubber,20 providing the clay is
modi"ed "rst and poly(N-vinylcarbazole)-clay hybrids
can be made by cationic polymerization of poly(N-vinyl-
carbazole) directly into the clay.21 Hesegawa et al. used
a twin screw extruder to produce a polypropylene-clay
hybrid from a modi"ed polypropylene oligomer and
modi"ed clay.22 As extrusion is a classical polymer pro-
cessing technique this may prove to be a low cost alterna-
tive to other less traditional methods.
Predicting whether or not a polymer-silicate hybrid
will form is not straight forward, as a wide variety of
factors in#uence the outcome. These include energy cha-
nges arising from the con"nement of the polymer within
the silicate, the expansion of the spaces between the
layers of the silicate, and those associated with inter-
molecular interaction between silicate surface, tethered
chain and polymer. With a view to improving predicta-
bility, attempts have been made to model the behaviour
of hybrids that form as a result of direct melt intercala-
tion with organically modi"ed clays23 and assess the
parameters required to favour intercalation.24 Progress
in this area will make the prospect of developing
nanocomposites as "re retardants more feasible.
A major problem when synthesising these types of
nanocomposites is the characterization of the product.
The most commonly used and powerful techniques for
structural characterization are x-ray di!raction11 and
transmission electron microscopy,25 but small and wide
angle x-ray scattering26 as well as small angle neutron
scattering27 are occasionally employed.
X-ray di!raction allows the determination of the
spaces between structural layers of the silicate utilizing
Intercalation and delamination change the dimensions
of the gaps between the silicate layers, so an increase in
layer distance indicates a nanocomposite has formed.
A reduction in the di!raction angle corresponds to an
increase in the silicate layer distance. Figure 5 shows
clearly the change in 2h from the montmorillonite to the
Work carried out recently at the University of
Greenwich has investigated the use of x-ray di!raction
with pyrolysis-mass spectrometry. When alkyl am-
monium salts are partially replaced by polyethylene ox-
ide in a clay nanocomposite, pyrolysis-mass spectro-
metry shows quite complex and interesting behaviour
(see Fig. 6), which suggests the presence of di!erent ad-
sorption sites in the composite, possibly loosely adsorbed
sites and more strongly intercalated sites.28 The "rst
peak in the temperature range 100}1603C appears to be
associated with the pyrolysis of the loosely bound cetyl
alkyl ammonium salt to produce C16 hydrocarbon,
a characteristic fragmentation pattern, C6H`11 being the
dominant ion, and a strong peak at m/z 58 attributed to
amine). Two further peaks are associated with this amine
loss at 2103}2203C and at 270}2803C, although interest-
ingly the hydrocarbon loss is much stronger in the latter.
In the latter peak the breakdown of polyethylene oxide to
dimer and trimer fragments is also observed, consistent
with the disintegration of the intercalate. Although
this study is ongoing, it does suggest that pyrolysis-mass
spectrometry may prove a useful tool to aid our under-
standing of interlayer bonding in nanocomposites.
Transmission electron micrography has been used ex-
tensively, by several research groups. When nanocom-
posites have formed the intersections of the silicate sheets

are seen as dark lines which are the cross sections of the
silicate layers, measuring 1 nm thick.29,11 This method
has also been used to study the nanocomposites after
combustion, typically the char can be seen as stacks of
1 nm dark lines in a multilayered structure.30
Many other analytical tools which have been em-
ployed to support these primary methods, either com-
pare the properties of the nanocomposite with those of
a mixture containing the same constituents or are used to
nanocomposite and the mixture.
The development of radiative gasi"cation19 techniques
has increased our understanding of the condensed phase
decomposition processes of pyrolysis. Using similar ap-
paratus to cone calorimetery this method allows pyroly-
sis in a nitrogen atmosphere at heat #uxes similar to
those found in "res. As well as establishing the mass loss
of a sample a video camera records both the pyrolysis
and charring phenomena. The process of char formation
by radiative gasi"cation can di!er for a polymer and the
corresponding polymer nanocomposite. (Fig. 7)
Thermogravimetric analysis and di!erential scanning
calorimetry has proved to be particularly informative in
understanding changes in "re performance. Thermog-
ravimetric analysis can yield information on the thermal
stability and the amount of organic material incorpor-
ated into the nanocomposite, while di!erential scanning
calorimetry can give a measure of the strength of the
The in#uence of the inorganic matrix on the organic
polymer is apparent in Fig. 8 where the major weight loss
is delayed from 4153C for the cashew nut shell liquid to
4553C in the nanocomposite. The amount of CNSL in
the intercalate can be quanti"ed from the magnitude of
the weight loss in this region (ca 20%).
A peak heat #ow of approximately 10 W/g for CNSL is
reduced to 1.0 W/g in the nanocomposite about half the
expected value for this composition. The exotherm in the
nanocomposite is also broader than for the CNSL,
D. PORTER, E. METCALFE AND M. J. K. THOMAS
Copyright ( 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Figure 7. Diagram of radiative gasiUcation apparatus.
Figure 8. TGA in air at 103C/min!1 (a) montmorillonite (b) cashew nut shell liquid (c) montmorillonite and cashew nut shell liquid
nanocomposite. (Costas Tovil, University of Greenwich, 1998, private communication).
showing that the thermal stability of the CNSL is clearly
a!ected by the inorganic matrix. (Fig. 9)
NMR linewidths have been used to study cation sites
within nanocomposites.31 Fourier transform infrared
spectroscopy32 may be able to identify changes between
the bonding in a mixture and the bonding in a related
nanocomposite, but as these variations are minute even
when intercalation has taken place, at present it is an
unreliable method of characterization in most cases.
These subsidiary methods have only been used to con-
"rm the evidence from the primary methods to date, but
building a clearer picture of the changes that occur when
a nanocomposite forms is important as it not only helps
to characterize the material, but in principle could indi-
cate novel methods of synthesis.
The "re retardant properties of a range of polymer-
silicate nanocomposites, including nylon, polypropylene
and polystyrene hybrids, have been investigated. Evid-
ence for their "re retardancy comes from a variety sour-
Key evidence for the resilience of polymer-silicate
nanocomposites to combustion includes:
f An increase in heat distortion temperature.
f An increase in peak heat release temperature.
f A decrease in char permeability.

montmorillonite and cashew nut shell liquid nanocomposite. (Costas Tovil, University of Greenwich, 1998, private comunica-
Heat release rate data3 for polymers and polymer}clay hybrids

The increase in heat distortion temperature is men-
tioned in several research papers. The Toyota group
found the heat distortion temperature of nylon-silicate
made by lactam cation exchange increased with clay con-
tent. When 5.3% clay was incorporated, in the pure nylon-6
polymer,18 the heat distortion temperature was found to
increase from 653C for nylon to 1643C for the hybrid.
The peak heat release rate is known to be an important
factor in predicting "re hazard. At NIST comparisons
were made between the heat release rates of pure polymers
and polymer}clay hybrids, when exposed to a 35 kW/m2
heat #ux as measured by cone calorimetry. The results are
shown in Table 2. At low clay concentrations the
reduction in peak heat release rate (*) was proportional
to the fraction of clay used.30
Transmission electron microscopy was used to study
char formation of nylon-6 and nylon-6-clay hybrids.30
When heated, but before ignition, a thin layer of char
formed on both samples. The nylon-6 char layer broke
down early in combustion but the nanocomposite char
did not fracture. Instead, a tough layer of char with
a multi-layered silicate structure grew and survived
throughout combustion. As a result, the dimensions
combustion, and the low permeability of the char
D. PORTER, E. METCALFE AND M. J. K. THOMAS
Copyright ( 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
limited the movement of both gases and liquids through
The rate of mass loss from a polymer-silicate hybrid
when exposed to heat is signi"cantly reduced compared
with the pure polymer.19 This di!erence comes into e!ect
after a couple of minutes of combustion, when the surface
of the silicate-polymer has had time to form char on the
Lee et al.10 found evidence of self-extinguishing prop-
erties in polyetherimide-silicate hybrids. The poly-
etherimide continued burning but the nanocomposite
became highly charred and stopped burning after the
It is generally considered that the nanocomposite
structure a!ects the char by increasing both its barrier
properties and reinforcing its structure, and that this is
the key to understanding the improvement in combus-
tion properties. The char insulates the underlying poly-
mer from heat and forms a barrier, slowing the escape of
the volatile gases produced by polymer degradation.
Both these actions interfere with the combustion cycle by
reducing the amount of fuel available for burning.
Commercially available "re retardants varying from "l-
lers such as alumina trihydrate to additives containing
halogens can be highly e!ective. They function through
a number of mechanisms on widely di!ering kinds of
materials, and although e!ective at reducing #ammabil-
ity they can have other disadvantages. The mechanical
properties of the polymer can be altered a!ecting the
quality of the product. Concerns over the environmental
impact have made halogen containing materials a less
popular option in many countries. Since 1996, major
American, European and Japanese producers of bro-
minated #ame retardants have been obliged to commit to
a voluntary risk reduction programme for many of their
products, coordinated by the OECD.33 The addition of
many "re retardants also increases the production of soot
and carbon monoxide during combustion.
The initial tests on silicate-polymer nanocomposites
show they have distinct advantages over traditional "re
retardants in these problem areas.
Relatively low concentrations of silicate are necessary
in nanocomposites, compared with the amount of addi-
tive used in traditional "ller systems allowing silicate-
polymer nanocomposites to be processed by extrusion,
injection moulding and casting in just the same way as
un"lled polymers. In contrast the high level of traditional
"llers incorporated often interfere with these processes.
The commercial advantages resulting from the low per-
cent of added silicate are therefore the lower cost and the
Polymer-silicate nanocomposites are
mentally friendly alternative to some types of "re retar-
dants, as they contain no additional halogens, phos-
phates or aromatics above those contained within the
polymer. The silicate remains intact at very high temper-
atures. In addition, whereas many "re retardant com-
pounds, particularly "ller types, increase the carbon
monoxide and soot levels produced during combustion,
no similar increases have been observed with nanocom-
Traditional "llers very often severely degrade the phys-
ical properties of the polymer or discolour it. A perhaps
surprising feature of polymer-silicate nanocomposites is
the improvement in many of the physical properties of
the polymer,35 including an increase in the dynamic
storage modulus of an intercalated polypropylene-clay
hybrid, compared with polypropylene,36 and an increase
in the tensile strength of a nylon-6-clay hybrid, compared
with nylon-6.35 The polymer colour is also unchanged.37
Although most research into nanocomposite "re retar-
dants is currently based on the layered silicate-polymer
structures discussed above, some alternatives are being
Nanocomposites based on matrix of polymethacrylate
and functionalized silica particles have been formed by in
situ free radical polymerization. Both the mechanical and
thermal properties of these composite have been investi-
Silicon-based interpenetrating polymer networks have
shown improved thermal and mechanical properties over
the polymer.39 These nanocomposites structures are con-
structed from stable Si-O and Si-C units intermingled
with the polymer, in a cross-linked system of silicic acid
and silicon containing polymer. The decomposition tem-
perature of the composite is related to the miscibility of
the components, the more miscible the components are,
the more thermally stable the composite.
Although still a relatively new area of development, the
silicate-polymer nanocomposites show great promise as
"re retardant materials. Not only can the combustion
properties of material be modi"ed but in many cases the
mechanical properties are also improved.
The "re retardant mechanisms of silicate-polymer
nanocomposites appear to hinge on changes in char
formation and strength. To date a number of silicates-
polymers have been shown to perform well in laboratory
use these materials commercially. But whether these
silicate-polymer nanocomposites structures can be syn-
thesized from a wider range of polymeric materials or
survive in extreme environments remains to be seen.
Much more research needs to be carried out to establish
their applications and limitations as "re retardants. The
potential synergy between traditional #ame retardants
and nanocomposites could further extend the uses of
nanocomposites as "re retardants.
